title: Meet GPT-Red: an LLM super-hacker OpenAI built to make its models safer
gist: OpenAI built an internal, unreleased LLM called GPT-Red that automates red-team hacking of its own models via a self-play training loop; the company says training against it made newly released flagship model GPT-5.6 far more robust than GPT-5 (fewer than 23% of GPT-Red's strongest attacks succeeded on GPT-5.6 versus over 90% on GPT-5), and GPT-Red discovered a novel 'fake chain of thought' prompt-injection technique.

themes.theme: GPT-Red as an automated adversarial training system

insights.claim: OpenAI built an LLM called GPT-Red, kept internal and not released publicly, that automates red-teaming (a safety-testing process normally done by human testers) by engaging in a self-play loop against OpenAI's other models, where GPT-Red tries to attack a target model and the target tries to defend itself, with both sides improving over many rounds inside a simulated 'dojo' covering scenarios like web browsing, email/calendar access, and code editing.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: A new automated adversarial-training pipeline for LLM security that could become a template other labs adopt, relevant to evaluating AI safety-tooling investment and OpenAI's competitive moat in model robustness.
insights.quote: OpenAI’s researchers took an LLM that had not been trained as a hacker and set it up in what’s known as a self-play loop with several other models. Its goal was to try to attack the other models; their goal was to try to defend themselves.

insights.claim: OpenAI says training its newly released flagship model GPT-5.6 against GPT-Red made it the company's most robust release yet, focusing especially on defending against prompt injection attacks, in which a hacker hides instructions in text an LLM encounters (such as code or a webpage) to make it act against its developers' or users' intent.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Adversarial self-play training is being credited as a direct driver of a flagship model release's security posture — a signal that automated red-teaming is becoming a core part of frontier model release pipelines, not just an auxiliary QA step.
insights.quote: OpenAI says that training it against GPT-Red made the model its most robust release yet.

insights.claim: OpenAI found that when it tested the strongest attacks GPT-Red had devised, more than 90% worked against GPT-5 (released August 2025) but fewer than 23% worked against the new GPT-5.6, a claimed roughly fourfold-plus reduction in successful attack rate between the two flagship model generations.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: A concrete, quantified security-robustness metric investors can use to compare successive OpenAI model releases and judge whether the company's safety claims are substantiated by measurable data rather than marketing language.
insights.quote: more than 90% of them worked against GPT-5 (released in August last year), and fewer than 23% worked against the new GPT-5.6.

insights.claim: When OpenAI reran a 2025 experiment in which human red-teamers tried to find weaknesses in an earlier GPT-5 version, GPT-Red was more successful at finding effective attacks than the human testers had been.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Direct evidence that an automated system now outperforms human red-teamers on at least this benchmark, suggesting AI-driven security testing could displace or heavily augment human red-teaming labor in AI safety workflows.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "OpenAI tested how good an attacker GPT-Red was"

insights.claim: OpenAI tested GPT-Red against Vendy, a vending-machine agent built by Andon Labs to benchmark real-world agent performance, and GPT-Red was able to hack it to change the prices of items for sale and cancel a customer's order.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: A concrete demonstrated real-world exploit against a commercial agentic system, illustrating tangible operational/fraud risk for companies deploying autonomous LLM agents in transactional settings (e-commerce, pricing, order management).
insights.quote: GPT-Red was able to hack Vendy to make it change the prices of items on sale and cancel a customer’s order.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "OpenAI also tested GPT-Red against Vendy"

themes.theme: Novel attack discovery: fake chain of thought

insights.claim: GPT-Red discovered a previously unseen type of prompt injection attack that OpenAI calls a 'fake chain of thought,' in which a fake entry is inserted into another model's internal reasoning trace to trick it into acting on spoofed information as though it had already verified it.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: A novel, named attack vector that other model providers and security teams should now specifically test for, since chain-of-thought reasoning traces are widely used across the industry and may share this vulnerability.
insights.quote: It’s like if I told you that 1+1=3 and that you have verified this already... The model’s like, ‘Oh, okay, of course,’ and it just spits out 3.

insights.claim: GPT-Red is not yet strong at attacks requiring multi-turn back-and-forth conversation with a target or at using images to carry out prompt injection attacks — areas where human attackers still have an advantage — so OpenAI continues to rely on human red-teamers to catch what GPT-Red misses.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Defines the current boundary of AI-automated security testing versus human expertise, useful for calibrating how much AI red-teaming can currently substitute for headcount in security teams.
insights.quote: It is not great at figuring out attacks that involve a back-and-forth conversation between hacker and target, something that human attackers would have few problems with. It is also not yet that great at using images

insights.claim: OpenAI says it will not release GPT-Red publicly and believes it is stronger than any copycat a competitor could easily build, citing more than a year of development backed by the compute resources of one of the world's richest companies.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: OpenAI is positioning its adversarial-testing capability itself as a defensible, non-commoditized asset — a compute-and-time moat argument relevant to assessing OpenAI's durable security/safety differentiation versus rivals.
insights.quote: It’s not a trivial thing that someone could easily do—you know, just go and train a super-attacker using this idea.

insights.claim: Jessica Ji, a senior research analyst at Georgetown's Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET), says OpenAI's self-play red-teaming approach is a good one with promising results, while stressing that human expertise remains important and that it would be useful to know precisely where human testing is still most needed.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
entities: Georgetown Center for Security and Emerging Technology
insights.so_what: An independent AI-security researcher's endorsement lends outside credibility to OpenAI's self-play red-teaming claims, though she flags that the human/AI division of labor in security testing is still being worked out.
insights.quote: The results look very promising... I think human expertise will still be very important... It would be really useful to be able to distinguish where human testing is most needed.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "Jessica Ji, a senior research analyst"

top_entities: Georgetown Center for Security and Emerging Technology
